why-prompts-matter.instruction: Write a vague prompt, then rewrite it with specificity. Show both versions to see the difference context makes.
why-prompts-matter.hint: Vague: Write me a cover letter. | Better: Write a cover letter for a senior UX designer applying to Spotify. Highlight 5 years of mobile app experience and a passion for music tech. Tone: confident but not arrogant. Under 300 words.
why-prompts-matter.goodExample: Write a cover letter for a senior UX designer applying to Spotify. Highlight 5 years of mobile app experience and a passion for music tech. Tone: confident but not arrogant. Under 300 words.
why-prompts-matter.successMessage: You just proved why prompts matter — the same request with context gets dramatically better results.

why-prompts-matter.instruction: Rewrite this weak prompt into something that would actually get a useful result: 'Tell me about marketing'
why-prompts-matter.hint: Explain 5 low-budget digital marketing strategies for a new Etsy shop selling handmade candles. Focus on tactics that cost under $50/month. Include expected time investment for each.
why-prompts-matter.goodExample: Explain 5 low-budget digital marketing strategies for a new Etsy shop selling handmade candles. Focus on tactics that cost under $50/month. Include expected time investment for each.
why-prompts-matter.successMessage: A specific audience, a concrete goal, and clear constraints — that's what turns a Google search into an AI conversation.

the-anatomy-of-a-great-prompt.instruction: Write a prompt that includes all 4 elements: (1) Role/context, (2) Task, (3) Constraints, (4) Output format.
the-anatomy-of-a-great-prompt.placeholder: Include role, task, constraints, and format...
the-anatomy-of-a-great-prompt.hint: I'm a freelance copywriter pitching to a SaaS startup. Write a 150-word cold email introducing my services. Focus on how I help increase trial-to-paid conversion through better onboarding emails. Tone: professional but personable. Format: subject line + email body.
the-anatomy-of-a-great-prompt.goodExample: I'm a freelance copywriter pitching to a SaaS startup. Write a 150-word cold email introducing my services. Focus on how I help increase trial-to-paid conversion through better onboarding emails. Tone: professional but personable. Format: subject line + email body.
the-anatomy-of-a-great-prompt.successMessage: All 4 elements present. This is the anatomy of a prompt that gets results on the first try.

the-anatomy-of-a-great-prompt.instruction: Look at these two prompts. Which one would get a better result, and why? Write the letter (A or B) and a one-sentence explanation.
the-anatomy-of-a-great-prompt.hint: B, because it specifies the audience, format, and constraints — giving the AI clear guardrails to work within.
the-anatomy-of-a-great-prompt.badExample:
A: Explain quantum computing.
B: Explain quantum computing to a 12-year-old using a video game analogy. Keep it under 100 words.
the-anatomy-of-a-great-prompt.goodExample: B, because it specifies the audience (12-year-old), the approach (video game analogy), and the length constraint (100 words) — all of which help the AI tailor its response perfectly.
the-anatomy-of-a-great-prompt.successMessage: Exactly right. Recognizing what makes a prompt strong is the first step to writing them naturally.

the-anatomy-of-a-great-prompt.instruction: Write a prompt where the CONSTRAINTS are the most important part. The limitations should shape the entire output.
the-anatomy-of-a-great-prompt.placeholder: A prompt where constraints do the heavy lifting...
the-anatomy-of-a-great-prompt.hint: Write a product description for wireless earbuds. Constraints: exactly 50 words, no technical jargon, must be understandable by a grandparent, must include a call to action, must NOT mention competitors.
the-anatomy-of-a-great-prompt.badExample: Write a product description for earbuds
the-anatomy-of-a-great-prompt.goodExample: Write a product description for wireless earbuds. Exactly 50 words. No technical jargon — a grandparent should understand it. Include one call to action. Do NOT mention any competitor brands. Tone: warm and inviting.
the-anatomy-of-a-great-prompt.successMessage: Constraints are secret weapons. They force the AI to think creatively within boundaries — just like the best human writing.

context-is-everything.instruction: Write a prompt where you give Claude rich background context BEFORE stating the task. Paint the picture first.
context-is-everything.hint: Context: I run a small bakery that's been open for 6 months. We're profitable on weekends but slow on weekdays. Our best sellers are sourdough and cinnamon rolls. We're in a college town. Task: Suggest 5 weekday promotions that would appeal to students on a budget.
context-is-everything.badExample: How do I get more customers?
context-is-everything.goodExample: Context: I run a small bakery, open 6 months, profitable on weekends but slow weekdays. Best sellers: sourdough and cinnamon rolls. Located in a college town. Budget for marketing: $200/month. Task: Suggest 5 weekday promotions targeting students on a budget.
context-is-everything.successMessage: Rich context = relevant answers. The AI can't read your mind, but it can work wonders with your situation.

context-is-everything.instruction: This prompt lacks context: 'Help me prepare for my interview.' Add the context that would make the response actually useful.
context-is-everything.placeholder: Add who you are, what role, what company...
context-is-everything.hint: I'm a mid-level data analyst with 4 years of experience in retail. I have an interview next Tuesday at Target's corporate office for a Senior Analytics Manager role. Help me prepare: give me 5 likely behavioral questions, 3 technical questions about SQL and Tableau, and advice on how to discuss my experience leading a team of 2.

mustNotInclude: help me prepare for my interview

context-is-everything.badExample: Help me prepare for my interview
context-is-everything.goodExample: I'm a data analyst with 4 years of retail experience, interviewing Tuesday at Target for Senior Analytics Manager. Prepare me with: 5 behavioral questions, 3 technical questions (SQL/Tableau focus), and talking points for discussing my team leadership of 2 direct reports.
context-is-everything.successMessage: Now THAT's a prompt that will get you interview-ready. Context transforms generic advice into a personalized coaching session.

roles-and-personas.instruction: Assign Claude a specific expert persona. Include who they are, their expertise, and how they should communicate.
roles-and-personas.placeholder: You are a... who specializes in...
roles-and-personas.hint: You are a veteran restaurant consultant who has helped 200+ restaurants survive their first year. You're direct, you don't sugarcoat, and you back every recommendation with a real-world example. When I describe my restaurant concept, evaluate it honestly.
roles-and-personas.goodExample: You are a veteran restaurant consultant who has helped 200+ restaurants survive their first year. You're direct, never sugarcoat problems, and back every recommendation with a real-world example from your experience. When I describe my concept, evaluate it honestly — I need truth, not encouragement.
roles-and-personas.successMessage: A well-defined persona gives Claude a voice, a perspective, and a communication style. It's like hiring a specialist.

roles-and-personas.instruction: Write a prompt that uses a CONTRASTING persona — someone with an unexpected perspective for the topic.
roles-and-personas.placeholder: You are a [surprising role] helping with [topic]...
roles-and-personas.hint: You are a stand-up comedian who also has a PhD in economics. Explain inflation to me like you're doing a 5-minute comedy set. Make it genuinely funny but factually accurate. Include at least one callback joke.
roles-and-personas.badExample: Explain economics in a fun way
roles-and-personas.goodExample: You are a stand-up comedian with a PhD in economics. Explain inflation like you're doing a 5-minute comedy set at a college campus. Be genuinely funny AND factually accurate. Include at least one callback joke and one audience interaction moment.
roles-and-personas.successMessage: Contrasting personas unlock creative outputs. A comedian-economist explains differently than a textbook — and it's more memorable.

the-art-of-constraints.instruction: Write a prompt with at least 3 specific constraints: length, format, and content restrictions.
the-art-of-constraints.hint: Write a product launch email for a new fitness app. Constraints: under 100 words, exactly 3 paragraphs, must include one statistic, must NOT use the words 'revolutionary' or 'game-changing', end with a single-sentence CTA.
the-art-of-constraints.badExample: Write a launch email for my app
the-art-of-constraints.goodExample: Write a product launch email for a fitness app. Under 100 words. Exactly 3 paragraphs. Include one statistic about fitness app engagement. Do NOT use 'revolutionary' or 'game-changing.' End with a one-sentence call to action.
the-art-of-constraints.successMessage: Three constraints, three dimensions of quality control. Constraints don't limit creativity — they focus it.

the-art-of-constraints.instruction: Write a prompt that uses NEGATIVE constraints — tell Claude what to AVOID rather than what to do.
the-art-of-constraints.hint: Write a LinkedIn recommendation for a software engineer. Do NOT use buzzwords like 'rockstar,' 'ninja,' or '10x developer.' Avoid generic praise like 'hard worker' or 'team player.' Don't exceed 100 words. Never use exclamation points.
the-art-of-constraints.goodExample: Write a LinkedIn recommendation for a software engineer. Do NOT use: 'rockstar,' 'ninja,' '10x,' 'passionate,' 'hard worker,' or 'team player.' No exclamation points. Avoid cliches entirely. Under 100 words. Focus on one specific project outcome instead of generic praise.
the-art-of-constraints.successMessage: Negative constraints strip away the generic. What's left is specific, authentic, and memorable.

the-art-of-constraints.instruction: This prompt has NO constraints: 'Write me a blog post about productivity.' Add at least 4 constraints that would make the output useful.
the-art-of-constraints.placeholder: Add length, audience, tone, format constraints...
the-art-of-constraints.hint: Write a blog post about productivity for remote workers who are parents. Under 800 words. Include 5 actionable tips (not generic advice like 'make a to-do list'). Tone: empathetic, no toxic productivity culture. Format: H2 headers for each tip, one-sentence summary at the end.

mustNotInclude: write me a blog post about productivity

the-art-of-constraints.badExample: Write me a blog post about productivity
the-art-of-constraints.goodExample: Write an 800-word blog post about productivity for remote-working parents. 5 actionable tips (nothing generic like 'make a list'). Tone: empathetic, anti-hustle-culture. Format: H2 per tip, 2-sentence intro, one-line summary at end. Include one personal anecdote placeholder.
the-art-of-constraints.successMessage: Four constraints turned an infinite prompt into a focused assignment. This is how professionals write prompts.

output-formats-that-work.instruction: Write a prompt that requests a specific structured output format — table, numbered list, JSON, markdown, or template.
output-formats-that-work.hint: Compare 3 project management tools (Asana, Trello, Monday) for a 10-person marketing team. Format as a markdown table with columns: Tool, Best For, Price/User, Learning Curve (1-5), Standout Feature. Add a one-sentence recommendation at the end.
output-formats-that-work.goodExample: Compare Asana, Trello, and Monday.com for a 10-person marketing team. Markdown table with columns: Tool | Best For | Price/User/Mo | Learning Curve (1-5) | Standout Feature. Then a 2-sentence recommendation for my specific team size.
output-formats-that-work.successMessage: Specifying format upfront means no back-and-forth. You get exactly the structure you need on the first try.

output-formats-that-work.instruction: Write a prompt that asks Claude to output something you could directly paste into a real tool — email client, spreadsheet, slide deck, etc.
output-formats-that-work.placeholder: Create output I can paste directly into...
output-formats-that-work.hint: Create 5 rows of sample customer data I can paste directly into a Google Sheet. Columns: Name, Email, Signup Date, Plan (Free/Pro/Enterprise), Monthly Revenue. Use realistic but fake data. Format as tab-separated values so I can paste it directly.
output-formats-that-work.goodExample: Create 5 rows of sample customer data for a Google Sheet. Columns: Name, Email, Signup Date (2024), Plan (Free/Pro/Enterprise), Monthly Revenue ($). Realistic fake data. Tab-separated values so I can paste directly. Include a header row.
output-formats-that-work.successMessage: Paste-ready outputs eliminate manual formatting. This one trick saves hours per week.

iteration-and-refinement.instruction: Write a follow-up prompt that refines a previous result. Pretend Claude gave you a draft — now ask for specific improvements.
iteration-and-refinement.hint: That draft is too formal. Rewrite it in a conversational tone — like I'm texting a friend who asked for advice. Keep the same 5 tips but make each one a single sentence. Add an emoji at the start of each tip. Cut the intro to one sentence.
iteration-and-refinement.goodExample: That draft is too formal. Rewrite in a conversational tone — like texting a friend. Keep the same 5 tips but condense each to one sentence. Add an emoji per tip. Cut the intro to one line. Remove the conclusion entirely.
iteration-and-refinement.successMessage: Iteration is where the magic happens. Great prompt engineers don't get it perfect on try one — they refine with precision.

iteration-and-refinement.instruction: Write a prompt that asks Claude to give you 3 variations of something so you can pick the best one.
iteration-and-refinement.hint: Write 3 different opening lines for a fundraising email. Version A: emotional storytelling approach. Version B: statistics-driven urgency. Version C: direct ask with social proof. Each under 30 words. Label them clearly.
iteration-and-refinement.badExample: Write an opening line for my email
iteration-and-refinement.goodExample: Give me 3 opening lines for a fundraising email. A: emotional storytelling. B: statistics-driven urgency. C: direct ask with social proof. Each under 30 words. Label them Version A/B/C so I can mix and match.
iteration-and-refinement.successMessage: Asking for variations gives you options AND teaches you what styles resonate. It's prompt engineering meets creative direction.

common-mistakes.instruction: This prompt makes 3 common mistakes. Identify and fix all of them: 'Can you maybe try to write something about social media tips if you can? I think it should be kind of professional but also casual? Maybe like 500 words or so?'
common-mistakes.placeholder: Rewrite without hedging, vagueness, or contradictions...
common-mistakes.hint: Write a 500-word LinkedIn article with 5 actionable social media tips for B2B SaaS companies. Tone: professional with a conversational edge — think Harvard Business Review meets a podcast host. Include one real-world example per tip.
common-mistakes.badExample: Can you maybe try to write something about social media tips if you can? I think it should be kind of professional but also casual? Maybe like 500 words or so?
common-mistakes.goodExample: Write a 500-word LinkedIn article: 5 social media tips for B2B SaaS companies. Tone: professional with conversational warmth (think HBR meets a podcast). One real-world example per tip. End with a CTA to comment with their best tip.
common-mistakes.successMessage: You eliminated the three killers: hedging language, vague qualifiers, and contradictory instructions. Clean, confident prompts get clean, confident outputs.

common-mistakes.instruction: Fix this overloaded prompt that tries to do too much in one request: 'Write a business plan and marketing strategy and financial projections and competitor analysis and pitch deck outline for my new coffee shop'
common-mistakes.placeholder: Break it into a focused, achievable request...
common-mistakes.hint: Let's start with the competitor analysis. Identify 5 coffee shops within 2 miles of downtown Austin. For each, list: name, price range, unique selling point, weakness I could exploit. Format as a table. I'll use this to inform the rest of my business plan.
common-mistakes.badExample: Write a business plan and marketing strategy and financial projections and competitor analysis and pitch deck for my coffee shop
common-mistakes.goodExample: Start with a competitor analysis for my new coffee shop in downtown Austin. Table format: 5 competitors, their price range, unique selling point, and one weakness I could exploit. I'll use this to build my business plan next.
common-mistakes.successMessage: One focused request beats five vague ones. Break big projects into steps — each prompt builds on the last.

prompt-templates-for-work.instruction: Create a reusable prompt template with [PLACEHOLDERS] for a task you do regularly at work. Include at least 3 variables.
prompt-templates-for-work.hint: Write a [LENGTH]-word [CONTENT TYPE] about [TOPIC] for [AUDIENCE]. Tone: [TONE]. Include [NUMBER] key points. Format: [FORMAT]. Must mention [REQUIRED ELEMENT]. Do not include [EXCLUSION].
prompt-templates-for-work.goodExample: Write a [LENGTH]-word [CONTENT TYPE] about [TOPIC] for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Tone: [TONE STYLE]. Include [NUMBER] actionable tips. Format: [OUTPUT FORMAT]. Must reference [KEY PRODUCT/SERVICE]. Avoid [COMPETITOR NAMES].
prompt-templates-for-work.successMessage: Templates are productivity multipliers. Save this pattern and swap out the variables each time you use it.

prompt-templates-for-work.instruction: Build a 'meeting debrief' template that extracts key info from any meeting notes. Include sections for decisions, action items, and follow-ups.
prompt-templates-for-work.placeholder: Create a template that processes meeting notes...
prompt-templates-for-work.hint: From the meeting notes below, extract: (1) DECISIONS MADE — list each decision and who approved it. (2) ACTION ITEMS — table with columns: Task, Owner, Deadline. (3) OPEN QUESTIONS — anything unresolved. (4) FOLLOW-UPS NEEDED — who needs to be contacted and why. Keep the total summary under 300 words.
prompt-templates-for-work.goodExample: From these meeting notes, extract: 1) DECISIONS — each decision + who approved. 2) ACTION ITEMS — table: Task | Owner | Deadline | Priority. 3) OPEN QUESTIONS — unresolved items. 4) FOLLOW-UPS — who to contact and why. Under 300 words total.
prompt-templates-for-work.successMessage: This template works for ANY meeting. Save it as a snippet — you'll use it dozens of times.
